option: Shopify as the Full Storefront (Liquid themes)
best_for: A merchant wanting the fastest path to a fully working store — checkout, inventory, payments, and storefront all in one managed platform
avoid_when: A brand needing a highly custom, non-templated frontend experience beyond what Liquid theming comfortably supports
tradeoffs: Everything (hosting, PCI compliance, checkout, payments) is managed by Shopify, at the cost of frontend flexibility being bounded by the Liquid templating system

best_for: A brand wanting full control over the frontend experience (custom Next.js/etc. storefront) while keeping Shopify's backend (inventory, checkout, admin) as the commerce engine
avoid_when: A merchant wanting the fastest possible setup with no custom frontend work — hosted Liquid themes (EC018) get there faster
tradeoffs: Complete frontend freedom and the ability to build genuinely distinctive storefronts (pairs with the UI/UX module's distinctiveness focus), at the cost of building and maintaining a custom frontend instead of using a theme

option: Shopify-Hosted Checkout, Not a Custom-Built Checkout Flow
best_for: Nearly every Shopify integration — Shopify's checkout is PCI-compliant, conversion-optimized, and includes Shop Pay/accelerated checkout out of the box
avoid_when: A very specific enterprise plan (Shopify Plus) explicitly licensing checkout customization beyond the standard extensibility points
tradeoffs: Rebuilding checkout from scratch forfeits Shopify's PCI compliance, fraud protection, and conversion-tested UX for essentially no benefit on standard plans — use Shopify Functions/checkout extensibility for customization instead of a full rebuild

option: GraphQL Admin API Over the Legacy REST Admin API for New Integrations
best_for: New integrations being built today — Shopify's GraphQL Admin API is the actively developed, more efficient interface for reading/writing store data
avoid_when: Maintaining an existing REST-based integration where a full migration isn't yet justified by a specific need
tradeoffs: GraphQL lets you fetch exactly the fields needed in one request and is where Shopify ships new features first; the REST Admin API is in maintenance mode for many resource types

option: Verify Every Shopify Webhook's HMAC Signature
best_for: Every Shopify webhook endpoint — verify the X-Shopify-Hmac-SHA256 header against your app's shared secret before trusting the payload, same principle as EC006 for Stripe
avoid_when: Never applicable to skip — an unverified webhook endpoint can be triggered by anyone who discovers the URL, potentially spoofing an order/inventory event
tradeoffs: This is SEC038's webhook-signature-verification rule applied to Shopify specifically — cheap to implement, closes off a real spoofing vector entirely

best_for: Every webhook handler — Shopify may redeliver the same webhook (on timeout/error), so handlers must be safe to process the same event twice, using the event id or a similar dedup key
avoid_when: Never assume single delivery — this is EC007's Stripe idempotency guidance applied identically to Shopify webhooks
tradeoffs: Deduplicating on Shopify's webhook id (or a combination of topic + resource id + timestamp where no single id is provided) prevents a redelivered webhook from duplicating a side effect like re-sending an order confirmation

option: Product Schema Markup (schema.org Product) Kept in Sync With Shopify Inventory
best_for: Any Shopify storefront wanting Google Shopping/rich-result visibility — structured data on product pages (price, availability, reviews) must reflect real-time Shopify inventory, not a stale snapshot
avoid_when: A B2B or wholesale storefront not aiming for public search visibility — schema markup investment is lower priority there
tradeoffs: Stale structured data (showing 'in stock' for a sold-out product) actively hurts both SEO trust signals and user experience — this needs to be wired to real Shopify inventory state, not set-and-forget at launch

option: Shopify as the Single Source of Truth for Inventory, Not a Duplicated Count
best_for: Any integration touching inventory — read/write inventory through Shopify's API rather than maintaining a separately-synced inventory count that can drift
avoid_when: A genuinely offline/POS-integrated business already using Shopify POS to keep inventory synced automatically — that's still Shopify being the source of truth, just via a different sync path
tradeoffs: A duplicated, independently-maintained inventory count is a classic source of overselling (selling a product that's actually out of stock) — always read the live count from Shopify or a properly webhook-synced cache, never a manually maintained mirror

option: Shopify Functions for Custom Checkout/Discount Logic Over Custom Checkout Rebuilds
best_for: Custom business logic at checkout (custom discount rules, custom shipping logic, custom payment customizations) on eligible plans — Shopify Functions extend checkout without forfeiting EC020's PCI/conversion benefits
avoid_when: Logic needs are fully covered by Shopify's built-in discount/shipping configuration — don't reach for custom Functions before checking if native configuration already covers the need
tradeoffs: Extends checkout behavior within Shopify's managed, PCI-compliant checkout rather than requiring a full custom rebuild (EC020) — the right escape hatch for custom logic without losing the platform's built-in guarantees

option: Shopify App OAuth Flow for Third-Party App Access, Never Sharing Store Admin Credentials
best_for: Building an app or integration that needs store access — use Shopify's OAuth flow to get a scoped access token, never ask a merchant for their actual admin login credentials
avoid_when: Never applicable to skip — this is the only legitimate way a third-party integration should authenticate against a Shopify store
tradeoffs: Scoped, revocable access tokens mean a merchant can revoke your app's access without changing their own password, and your app never handles or stores their actual login credentials

option: Respect Shopify's API Rate Limits With Backoff, Not Aggressive Retries
best_for: Every integration calling the Shopify API — implement exponential backoff (BE071) when hitting rate-limit responses (leaky-bucket algorithm for REST, cost-based throttling for GraphQL), rather than retrying immediately in a tight loop
avoid_when: A very low-volume integration making infrequent calls well under any rate limit — still worth implementing defensively, but less urgent
tradeoffs: Aggressive retry-without-backoff behavior against a rate-limited API makes the throttling worse and can get an app's API access further restricted — proper backoff is both good citizenship and better for your own integration's reliability

option: Shopify as the Central Inventory/Order Hub for Multi-Channel Selling (Amazon, retail, etc.)
best_for: Merchants selling across multiple channels — route all channel orders and inventory updates through Shopify as the central system rather than reconciling separate systems manually
avoid_when: A single-channel storefront with no other sales channels to reconcile — this consideration doesn't apply yet
tradeoffs: Centralizing through Shopify (via its multi-channel integrations or a middleware/iPaaS tool) avoids the overselling and reconciliation headaches of treating each channel as an independent inventory source
